title: Intermittent Fasting Enhances Leptin Sensitivity via JMJD3-Mediated Epigenetic Regulation
keywords: high-fat diet induced obesity (60% fat, 10 weeks)

summary.hook: This poster tests whether intermittent fasting (IF) can restore leptin sensitivity in diet-induced obese mice by increasing hypothalamic leptin receptor (LepRb) expression through epigenetic regulation.
summary.summary_30s: This poster tests whether intermittent fasting (IF) can restore leptin sensitivity in diet-induced obese mice by increasing hypothalamic leptin receptor (LepRb) expression through epigenetic regulation. Male C57BL/6J mice were made obese on a high-fat diet, then assigned to ad libitum feeding or alternate-day fasting for 10–20 days depending on experiment, and leptin responsiveness was assessed via leptin injections, food intake/body weight, and hypothalamic signaling (pSTAT3). The authors report that IF improves leptin sensitivity without changing overall body weight or fat mass, increases hypothalamic LepRb and leptin signaling, and is associated with reduced repressive histone methylation (H3K27 methylation) at the LepRb promoter, mediated by JMJD3.
talktrack_2m: Focus: To investigate the molecular mechanism behind body weight management through IF.

Approach: Population: 7-week-old male C57BL/6J mice induced with obesity using high-fat diet (HFD). Primary outcome: Lepin sensitivity / leptin signaling (food intake and body weight responses to leptin; hypothalamic pSTAT3), LepRb expression.
Finding: IF improves leptin sensitivity in obese mice without altering overall body weight or fat mass.
Why it matters: * Authors’ stated conclusions: (1) IF improves leptin sensitivity in obese mice without altering overall body weight or fat mass.
key_numbers: 7-week-old male C57BL/6J mice induced with obesity using high-fat diet (HFD).
Sample sizes ranged from n=4–8 per group depending on experiment; significance markers reported as p<0.05 and *p<0.01.
Male C57BL/6J mice were made obese on a high-fat diet, then assigned to ad libitum feeding or alternate-day fasting for 10–20 days depending on experiment, and leptin responsive...
The authors report that IF improves leptin sensitivity without changing overall body weight or fat mass, increases hypothalamic LepRb and leptin signaling, and is associated wit...
takeaways: IF improves leptin sensitivity in obese mice without altering overall body weight or fat mass.
IF enhances hypothalamic LepRb expression and leptin signaling (increased STAT3 phosphorylation and target gene activation).
Epigenetic analysis shows reduced H3K27 methylation at the LepRb promoter, suggesting transcriptional activation under IF.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What statistical tests were used for each figure panel, and were assumptions checked?

what_to_ask.question: In Figure 1, what were the absolute differences in food intake after leptin injections between AL and IF groups?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: Were multiple comparisons controlled across the many endpoints (body weight, fat content, pSTAT3, multiple genes, ChIP marks)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: If IF improves leptin sensitivity without weight loss, what does that imply for early obesity interventions focused on hormonal responsiveness?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
